content.content:
1→# Slice: ActiveTaskList
     2→
     3→## Pattern
     4→
     5→**View** (State View / Read Model)
     6→
     7→## Summary
     8→
     9→A read model that projects all events into an ordered list of active
    10→(incomplete, non-deleted) tasks. This powers the main view of the application.
    11→
    12→## Diagram
    13→
    14→```mermaid
    15→flowchart LR
    16→    E1["TaskAdded"]:::event
    17→    E2["TaskCompleted"]:::event
    18→    E3["TaskReopened"]:::event
    19→    E4["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event
    20→    E5["TaskReprioritized"]:::event
    21→    E6["TaskDeleted"]:::event
    22→    VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view
    23→
    24→    E1 --> VIEW
    25→    E2 --> VIEW
    26→    E3 --> VIEW
    27→    E4 --> VIEW
    28→    E5 --> VIEW
    29→    E6 --> VIEW
    30→
    31→    classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    32→    classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
    33→```
    34→
    35→## Read Model: ActiveTaskList
    36→
    37→### Fields
    38→
    39→| Field | Type | Source Event(s) | Description |
    40→|-------|------|----------------|-------------|
    41→| tasks | list | (all events) | Ordered list of active tasks |
    42→| tasks[].task_id | identifier | TaskAdded.task_id | Unique task identifier |
    43→| tasks[].description | text | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | Current description (latest value) |
    44→| tasks[].position | number | TaskAdded.position, TaskReprioritized.position, TaskReopened (top) | Current priority position |
    45→| completed_count | number | TaskCompleted (+1), TaskReopened (-1), TaskDeleted (if completed, -1) | Count of completed tasks for badge |
    46→
    47→### Filters
    48→
    49→- Only tasks where **completed = false** AND **deleted = false**.
    50→
    51→### Ordering
    52→
    53→- By position ascending. Position 1 = highest priority (top of list).
    54→
    55→### Event Processing Rules
    56→
    57→| Event | Effect on Read Model |
    58→|-------|---------------------|
    59→| TaskAdded | Add new task at the bottom of the list (highest position number) |
    60→| TaskCompleted | Remove task from active list. Increment completed_count. |
    61→| TaskReopened | Add task back to active list at position 1 (top). Shift existing tasks down. Decrement completed_count. |
    62→| TaskDescriptionChanged | Update description of matching task. |
    63→| TaskReprioritized | Move task to new position. Shift other tasks to accommodate. |
    64→| TaskDeleted | Remove task from active list. If task was completed, decrement completed_count. |
    65→
    66→### Information Completeness Check
    67→
    68→| Display Element | Source |
    69→|----------------|--------|
    70→| Task description text | TaskAdded.description / TaskDescriptionChanged.description |
    71→| Task order | TaskAdded.position / TaskReprioritized.position / TaskReopened (top) |
    72→| Completed count badge | TaskCompleted / TaskReopened / TaskDeleted |
    73→| Empty state message | Derived: tasks list is empty |
    74→
    75→All fields trace to events. No missing data.
    76→
    77→## Wireframe
    78→
    79→```
    80→+--------------------------------------------------+
    81→|  My Tasks                        [Completed (3)]  |
    82→+--------------------------------------------------+
    83→|                                                    |
    84→|  [ What needs to be done?            ] [+ Add]    |
    85→|                                                    |
    86→|  ::  Buy groceries                    [v] [ x ]   |
    87→|  ::  Call the dentist                 [v] [ x ]   |
    88→|  ::  Fix the leaky faucet            [v] [ x ]   |
    89→|  ::  Write thank-you notes           [v] [ x ]   |
    90→|                                                    |
    91→+--------------------------------------------------+
    92→```
    93→
    94→## GWT Scenarios
    95→
    96→### Scenario: TaskAdded adds task to the bottom of an empty list
    97→
    98→**Given** (current projection state):
    99→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [], completed_count: 0 }
   100→
   101→**When** (event to process):
   102→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }
   103→
   104→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   105→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }
   106→
   107→---
   108→
   109→### Scenario: TaskAdded adds task to the bottom of a populated list
   110→
   111→**Given** (current projection state):
   112→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 0 }
   113→
   114→**When** (event to process):
   115→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }
   116→
   117→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   118→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   119→
   120→---
   121→
   122→### Scenario: TaskCompleted removes task from active list and increments completed count
   123→
   124→**Given** (current projection state):
   125→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 1 }
   126→
   127→**When** (event to process):
   128→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" }
   129→
   130→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   131→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 2 }
   132→
   133→---
   134→
   135→### Scenario: TaskReopened adds task to top of list and decrements completed count
   136→
   137→**Given** (current projection state):
   138→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 2 }], completed_count: 2 }
   139→
   140→**When** (event to process):
   141→- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" }
   142→
   143→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   144→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 1 }
   145→
   146→**Note:** The reopened task's description ("Call the dentist") must be resolved from prior events. Existing tasks shift down (positions increment by 1).
   147→
   148→---
   149→
   150→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged updates description of an active task
   151→
   152→**Given** (current projection state):
   153→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }
   154→
   155→**When** (event to process):
   156→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" }
   157→
   158→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   159→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }
   160→
   161→---
   162→
   163→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged for a completed task does not affect active list
   164→
   165→**Given** (current projection state):
   166→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   167→
   168→**When** (event to process):
   169→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist office" }
   170→
   171→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   172→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   173→
   174→---
   175→
   176→### Scenario: TaskReprioritized moves task to a new position (higher priority)
   177→
   178→**Given** (current projection state):
   179→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   180→
   181→**When** (event to process):
   182→- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-003", position: 1 }
   183→
   184→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   185→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   186→
   187→---
   188→
   189→### Scenario: TaskDeleted removes an active task from the list
   190→
   191→**Given** (current projection state):
   192→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 1 }
   193→
   194→**When** (event to process):
   195→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-002" }
   196→
   197→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   198→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   199→
   200→---
   201→
   202→### Scenario: TaskDeleted for a completed task decrements completed count but does not change active list
   203→
   204→**Given** (current projection state):
   205→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 2 }
   206→
   207→**When** (event to process):
   208→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-005" }
   209→
   210→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   211→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   212→
   213→**Note:** The projection must track whether a deleted task was completed in order to correctly adjust the completed_count.
   214→

## Project Context

**Project type:** Elixir/Phoenix 1.8 web application (currently a fresh Phoenix app with no event sourcing dependencies)

- Domain discovery complete

- NO event sourcing libraries yet
- NO `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` yet

## Domain Overview

Single-user prioritized todo list. One actor (User), no authentication. Events:
- TaskAdded, TaskCompleted, TaskReopened, TaskDescriptionChanged, TaskReprioritized, TaskDeleted

Commands: AddTask, CompleteTask, ReopenTask, ChangeTaskDescription, ReprioritizeTask, DeleteTask

Read Models: ActiveTaskList (main view), CompletedTaskList (completed view)

No automations, no external integrations.

1. **Technology Stack** — ES framework, event store, projections, UUID generation
2. **Domain Boundaries** — Aggregate design, context module API, domain types
3. **Integration Approaches** — LiveView integration pattern
4. **Cross-Cutting Concerns** — Testing strategy, error handling

2. Get my choice
3. Invoke `/sdlc:adr decide <topic>` (via the Skill tool) to update ARCHITECTURE.md and create an ADR PR

Each decision = one ADR PR. Do NOT batch multiple decisions.

- This is an Elixir/Phoenix project — all technology choices should be from the Elixir ecosystem
- The project uses Nix for development environment, docker-compose for PostgreSQL
- Tests should use in-memory adapters where possible (fast tests)
- The project already uses git-spice for stacked PRs (see `.claude/sdlc.yaml`)
- Refer to AGENTS.md at the project root for Phoenix-specific coding guidelines

content.content:
1→# Slice: CompletedTaskList
     2→
     3→## Pattern
     4→
     5→**View** (State View / Read Model)
     6→
     7→## Summary
     8→
     9→A read model that projects events into a list of completed (done, non-deleted)
    10→tasks. This powers the completed tasks view where users can review finished work
    11→and reopen or delete tasks.
    12→
    13→## Diagram
    14→
    15→```mermaid
    16→flowchart LR
    17→    E1["TaskAdded"]:::event
    18→    E2["TaskCompleted"]:::event
    19→    E3["TaskReopened"]:::event
    20→    E4["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event
    21→    E6["TaskDeleted"]:::event
    22→    VIEW["CompletedTaskList"]:::view
    23→
    24→    E1 --> VIEW
    25→    E2 --> VIEW
    26→    E3 --> VIEW
    27→    E4 --> VIEW
    28→    E6 --> VIEW
    29→
    30→    classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    31→    classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
    32→```
    33→
    34→## Read Model: CompletedTaskList
    35→
    36→### Fields
    37→
    38→| Field | Type | Source Event(s) | Description |
    39→|-------|------|----------------|-------------|
    40→| tasks | list | (filtered events) | List of completed tasks |
    41→| tasks[].task_id | identifier | TaskAdded.task_id | Unique task identifier |
    42→| tasks[].description | text | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | Current description (latest value) |
    43→
    44→### Filters
    45→
    46→- Only tasks where **completed = true** AND **deleted = false**.
    47→
    48→### Ordering
    49→
    50→- By completion time descending (most recently completed first). Completion
    51→  time is derived from the TaskCompleted event timestamp.
    52→
    53→### Event Processing Rules
    54→
    55→| Event | Effect on Read Model |
    56→|-------|---------------------|
    57→| TaskAdded | No effect (new tasks are not completed). |
    58→| TaskCompleted | Add task to completed list. |
    59→| TaskReopened | Remove task from completed list. |
    60→| TaskDescriptionChanged | Update description of matching task (if present in list). |
    61→| TaskDeleted | Remove task from completed list (if present). |
    62→
    63→### Information Completeness Check
    64→
    65→| Display Element | Source |
    66→|----------------|--------|
    67→| Task description text (with strikethrough) | TaskAdded.description / TaskDescriptionChanged.description |
    68→| Task ordering (most recent first) | TaskCompleted event timestamp |
    69→| Empty state | Derived: tasks list is empty |
    70→
    71→All fields trace to events. No missing data.
    72→
    73→## Wireframe
    74→
    75→```
    76→+--------------------------------------------------+
    77→|  [< Back to Tasks]              Completed Tasks   |
    78→+--------------------------------------------------+
    79→|                                                    |
    80→|  ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~       [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
    81→|  ~~Send the invoice~~           [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
    82→|  ~~Order new lightbulbs~~       [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
    83→|                                                    |
    84→+--------------------------------------------------+
    85→```
    86→
    87→## GWT Scenarios
    88→
    89→### Scenario: TaskAdded has no effect on completed list
    90→
    91→**Given** (current projection state):
    92→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }
    93→
    94→**When** (event to process):
    95→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }
    96→
    97→**Then** (resulting projection state):
    98→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }
    99→
   100→---
   101→
   102→### Scenario: TaskCompleted adds task to the completed list
   103→
   104→**Given** (current projection state):
   105→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }
   106→
   107→**When** (event to process):
   108→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" }
   109→
   110→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   111→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   112→
   113→**Note:** The task's description must be resolved from prior events (TaskAdded or TaskDescriptionChanged).
   114→
   115→---
   116→
   117→### Scenario: TaskCompleted adds most recent completion to the top
   118→
   119→**Given** (current projection state):
   120→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   121→
   122→**When** (event to process):
   123→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" }
   124→
   125→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   126→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   127→
   128→**Note:** Most recently completed task appears first (descending completion time).
   129→
   130→---
   131→
   132→### Scenario: TaskReopened removes task from completed list
   133→
   134→**Given** (current projection state):
   135→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   136→
   137→**When** (event to process):
   138→- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" }
   139→
   140→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   141→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   142→
   143→---
   144→
   145→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged updates description of a completed task
   146→
   147→**Given** (current projection state):
   148→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   149→
   150→**When** (event to process):
   151→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries and snacks" }
   152→
   153→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   154→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries and snacks" }] }
   155→
   156→---
   157→
   158→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged for a non-completed task does not affect completed list
   159→
   160→**Given** (current projection state):
   161→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   162→
   163→**When** (event to process):
   164→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet soon" }
   165→
   166→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   167→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   168→
   169→---
   170→
   171→### Scenario: TaskDeleted removes task from completed list
   172→
   173→**Given** (current projection state):

1→# Slice: ReopenTask 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user clicks [Reopen] on a completed task. The task is set back to 10→incomplete and placed at the top of the active list (highest priority position). 11→ 12→## Diagram 13→ 14→```mermaid 15→flowchart LR 16→ UI["Completed Tasks View
Reopen button"] 17→ CMD["ReopenTask"]:::command 18→ EVT["TaskReopened"]:::event 19→ VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 20→ VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view 21→ 22→ UI -->|"task_id"| CMD 23→ CMD --> EVT 24→ EVT --> VIEW1 25→ EVT --> VIEW2 26→ 27→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 28→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 29→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 30→``` 31→ 32→## Command: ReopenTask 33→ 34→| Field | Type | Description | 35→|-------|------|-------------| 36→| task_id | identifier | The completed task to reopen | 37→ 38→**Triggered by:** User clicks the [Reopen] button on a task in the completed 39→tasks view. 40→ 41→**Rejection conditions:** 42→- Task is not completed (already active). 43→ 44→## Event: TaskReopened 45→ 46→| Field | Type | Description | 47→|-------|------|-------------| 48→| task_id | identifier | The task that was reopened | 49→ 50→**Business rule:** The reopened task is placed at the top of the active list 51→(highest priority position). This differs from newly added tasks which go to 52→the bottom. Rationale: if a user reopens a task, it is likely urgent. 53→ 54→## Wireframe 55→ 56→``` 57→Before (Completed Tasks View): 58→ 59→+--------------------------------------------------+ 60→| [< Back to Tasks] Completed Tasks | 61→+--------------------------------------------------+ 62→| | 63→| ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 64→| ~~Send the invoice~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 65→| ~~Order new lightbulbs~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 66→| | 67→+--------------------------------------------------+ 68→ 69→User clicks [Reopen] on "Send the invoice": 70→ 71→+--------------------------------------------------+ 72→| [< Back to Tasks] Completed Tasks | 73→+--------------------------------------------------+ 74→| | 75→| ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 76→| ~~Order new lightbulbs~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 77→| | 78→+--------------------------------------------------+ 79→ 80→Active Task List now shows (navigating back): 81→ 82→+--------------------------------------------------+ 83→| My Tasks [Completed (2)] | 84→+--------------------------------------------------+ 85→| | 86→| :: Send the invoice [v] [ x ] | 87→| ^--- reopened task at TOP | 88→| :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | 89→| :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | 90→| | 91→+--------------------------------------------------+ 92→``` 93→ 94→## Read Model Impact 95→ 96→**ActiveTaskList:** Task appears at the top (highest priority). Completed count 97→decremented. 98→**CompletedTaskList:** Task removed from completed list. 99→ 100→## GWT Scenarios 101→ 102→### Scenario: Reopen a completed task (goes to top of active list) 103→ 104→**Given** (prior events): 105→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 106→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 107→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" } 108→ 109→**When** (command): 110→- ReopenTask { task_id: "task-002" } 111→ 112→**Then** (events produced): 113→- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" } 114→ 115→--- 116→ 117→### Scenario: Reject reopening a task that is already active 118→ 119→**Given** (prior events): 120→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 121→ 122→**When** (command): 123→- ReopenTask { task_id: "task-001" } 124→ 125→**Then** (error -- no events): 126→- Error: "Task is not completed" 127→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

1→# Slice: ChangeTaskDescription 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user clicks on a task's description text to activate inline editing, changes 10→the text, and confirms the edit (by pressing Enter or clicking away). The task's 11→description is updated. 12→ 13→## Diagram 14→ 15→```mermaid 16→flowchart LR 17→ UI["Active Task List
inline edit field"] 18→ CMD["ChangeTaskDescription"]:::command 19→ EVT["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event 20→ VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 21→ VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view 22→ 23→ UI -->|"task_id, new_description"| CMD 24→ CMD --> EVT 25→ EVT --> VIEW1 26→ EVT --> VIEW2 27→ 28→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 29→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 30→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 31→``` 32→ 33→## Command: ChangeTaskDescription 34→ 35→| Field | Type | Description | 36→|-------|------|-------------| 37→| task_id | identifier | The task to edit | 38→| new_description | text | The updated description (non-empty) | 39→ 40→**Triggered by:** User clicks on a task description in the active list, edits 41→the text inline, and confirms by pressing Enter or blurring the field. 42→ 43→**Rejection conditions:** 44→- New description is empty or blank. 45→- New description is identical to the current description (no change). 46→ 47→## Event: TaskDescriptionChanged 48→ 49→| Field | Type | Description | 50→|-------|------|-------------| 51→| task_id | identifier | The task whose description changed | 52→| description | text | The new description | 53→ 54→## Wireframe 55→ 56→``` 57→Before (normal display): 58→ 59→ :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] 60→ 61→User clicks on the description text: 62→ 63→ :: [Call the dentist office____] [v] [ x ] 64→ ^--- inline editing active, cursor in text field 65→ 66→User presses Enter or clicks away: 67→ 68→ :: Call the dentist office [v] [ x ] 69→ ^--- updated description displayed 70→``` 71→ 72→## Read Model Impact 73→ 74→**ActiveTaskList:** Task description updated (if task is active). 75→**CompletedTaskList:** Task description updated (if task is completed). 76→ 77→## GWT Scenarios 78→ 79→### Scenario: Change a task's description 80→ 81→**Given** (prior events): 82→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 } 83→ 84→**When** (command): 85→- ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist office" } 86→ 87→**Then** (events produced): 88→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" } 89→ 90→--- 91→ 92→### Scenario: Reject changing description to the same value (no change) 93→ 94→**Given** (prior events): 95→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 } 96→ 97→**When** (command): 98→- ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist" } 99→ 100→**Then** (error -- no events): 101→- Error: "Description is unchanged" 102→ 103→--- 104→ 105→### Scenario: Change description after it was previously changed 106→ 107→**Given** (prior events): 108→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 } 109→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" } 110→ 111→**When** (command): 112→- ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm" } 113→ 114→**Then** (events produced): 115→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm" } 116→ 117→--- 118→ 119→### Scenario: Reject changing description back to a previously changed value that matches current 120→ 121→**Given** (prior events): 122→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 } 123→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" } 124→ 125→**When** (command): 126→- ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist office" } 127→ 128→**Then** (error -- no events): 129→- Error: "Description is unchanged" 130→ 131→**Note:** Empty/blank description is prevented by the type system (NonEmptyString domain type), not by command rejection. No GWT scenario is needed for that case. 132→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

1→# Slice: ReprioritizeTask 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user drags a task from one position in the active list and drops it at a 10→new position. The task's priority changes relative to the other tasks. 11→ 12→## Diagram 13→ 14→```mermaid 15→flowchart LR 16→ UI["Active Task List
drag handle"] 17→ CMD["ReprioritizeTask"]:::command 18→ EVT["TaskReprioritized"]:::event 19→ VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 20→ 21→ UI -->|"task_id, new_position"| CMD 22→ CMD --> EVT 23→ EVT --> VIEW 24→ 25→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 26→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 27→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 28→``` 29→ 30→## Command: ReprioritizeTask 31→ 32→| Field | Type | Description | 33→|-------|------|-------------| 34→| task_id | identifier | The task being moved | 35→| new_position | number | The target position in the list | 36→ 37→**Triggered by:** User grabs a drag handle (::) and drops the task at a new 38→position in the active list. 39→ 40→**Rejection conditions:** 41→- Task dropped in the same position (no change). 42→ 43→## Event: TaskReprioritized 44→ 45→| Field | Type | Description | 46→|-------|------|-------------| 47→| task_id | identifier | The task that was moved | 48→| position | number | The new position in the priority-ordered list | 49→ 50→## Wireframe 51→ 52→``` 53→Before: 54→ 55→ :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] 56→ :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] 57→ :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] 58→ :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] 59→ 60→User drags "Fix the leaky faucet" to the top: 61→ 62→ :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] <-- moved here 63→ :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] 64→ :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] 65→ :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] 66→``` 67→ 68→## Read Model Impact 69→ 70→**ActiveTaskList:** Task order updated. The moved task appears at its new 71→position; other tasks shift to accommodate. 72→ 73→## GWT Scenarios 74→ 75→### Scenario: Move a task to a higher priority (lower position number) 76→ 77→**Given** (prior events): 78→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 79→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 80→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 } 81→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-004", description: "Write thank-you notes", position: 4 } 82→ 83→**When** (command): 84→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-003", new_position: 1 } 85→ 86→**Then** (events produced): 87→- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-003", position: 1 } 88→ 89→--- 90→ 91→### Scenario: Move a task to a lower priority (higher position number) 92→ 93→**Given** (prior events): 94→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 95→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 96→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 } 97→ 98→**When** (command): 99→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-001", new_position: 3 } 100→ 101→**Then** (events produced): 102→- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-001", position: 3 } 103→ 104→--- 105→ 106→### Scenario: Reject reprioritizing to the same position (no change) 107→ 108→**Given** (prior events): 109→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 110→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 111→ 112→**When** (command): 113→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-002", new_position: 2 } 114→ 115→**Then** (error -- no events): 116→- Error: "Task is already at this position" 117→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. 1→# Slice: DeleteTask 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user clicks the delete button on a task (from either the active list or the 10→completed tasks view). A confirmation dialog appears. If the user confirms, the 11→task is permanently removed from all views. 12→ 13→## Diagram 14→ 15→```mermaid 16→flowchart LR 17→ UI1["Active Task List
delete button"] 18→ UI2["Completed Tasks View
delete button"] 19→ DIALOG["Confirmation Dialog"] 20→ CMD["DeleteTask"]:::command 21→ EVT["TaskDeleted"]:::event 22→ VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 23→ VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view 24→ 25→ UI1 -->|"click x"| DIALOG 26→ UI2 -->|"click x"| DIALOG 27→ DIALOG -->|"confirm, task_id"| CMD 28→ CMD --> EVT 29→ EVT --> VIEW1 30→ EVT --> VIEW2 31→ 32→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 33→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 34→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 35→``` 36→ 37→## Command: DeleteTask 38→ 39→| Field | Type | Description | 40→|-------|------|-------------| 41→| task_id | identifier | The task to delete | 42→ 43→**Triggered by:** User clicks [x] on any task (active or completed), then 44→clicks [Delete] in the confirmation dialog. 45→ 46→**Rejection conditions:** 47→- User clicks [Cancel] in the confirmation dialog (command is never issued). 48→ 49→## Event: TaskDeleted 50→ 51→| Field | Type | Description | 52→|-------|------|-------------| 53→| task_id | identifier | The task that was deleted | 54→ 55→**Business rule:** Deletion is permanent from the UI perspective. The event 56→stream retains the full history, but the UI provides no way to restore a 57→deleted task. 58→ 59→## Wireframe 60→ 61→``` 62→User clicks [x] on "Fix the leaky faucet": 63→ 64→+--------------------------------------------------+ 65→| | 66→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 67→| | Delete Task? | | 68→| | | | 69→| | "Fix the leaky faucet" will be | | 70→| | permanently removed. | | 71→| | | | 72→| | [Cancel] [Delete] | | 73→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 74→| | 75→+--------------------------------------------------+ 76→ 77→If user clicks [Delete], the task disappears from the list. 78→If user clicks [Cancel], nothing changes. 79→``` 80→ 81→## Read Model Impact 82→ 83→**ActiveTaskList:** Task removed (if it was active). Remaining tasks may shift 84→positions. 85→**CompletedTaskList:** Task removed (if it was completed). 86→ 87→## GWT Scenarios 88→ 89→### Scenario: Delete an active task 90→ 91→**Given** (prior events): 92→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 93→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 94→ 95→**When** (command): 96→- DeleteTask { task_id: "task-002" } 97→ 98→**Then** (events produced): 99→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-002" } 100→ 101→--- 102→ 103→### Scenario: Delete a completed task 104→ 105→**Given** (prior events): 106→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 107→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" } 108→ 109→**When** (command): 110→- DeleteTask { task_id: "task-001" } 111→ 112→**Then** (events produced): 113→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-001" } 114→ 115→**Note:** The confirmation dialog is a UI concern. If the user clicks Cancel, the command is never issued, so there is no rejection scenario at the command level. Once the DeleteTask command is issued (after confirmation), it always succeeds. 116→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

1→defmodule TodoList.MixProject do
     2→  use Mix.Project
     3→
     4→  def project do
     5→    [
     6→      app: :todo_list,
     7→      version: "0.1.0",
     8→      elixir: "~> 1.15",
     9→      elixirc_paths: elixirc_paths(Mix.env()),
    10→      start_permanent: Mix.env() == :prod,
    11→      aliases: aliases(),
    12→      deps: deps(),
    13→      compilers: [:phoenix_live_view] ++ Mix.compilers(),
    14→      listeners: [Phoenix.CodeReloader]
    15→    ]
    16→  end
    17→
    18→  # Configuration for the OTP application.
    19→  #
    20→  # Type `mix help compile.app` for more information.
    21→  def application do
    22→    [
    23→      mod: {TodoList.Application, []},
    24→      extra_applications: [:logger, :runtime_tools]
    25→    ]
    26→  end
    27→
    28→  def cli do
    29→    [
    30→      preferred_envs: [precommit: :test]
    31→    ]
    32→  end
    33→
    34→  # Specifies which paths to compile per environment.
    35→  defp elixirc_paths(:test), do: ["lib", "test/support"]
    36→  defp elixirc_paths(_), do: ["lib"]
    37→
    38→  # Specifies your project dependencies.
    39→  #
    40→  # Type `mix help deps` for examples and options.
    41→  defp deps do
    42→    [
    43→      {:phoenix, "~> 1.8.3"},
    44→      {:phoenix_ecto, "~> 4.5"},
    45→      {:ecto_sql, "~> 3.13"},
    46→      {:postgrex, ">= 0.0.0"},
    47→      {:phoenix_html, "~> 4.1"},
    48→      {:phoenix_live_reload, "~> 1.2", only: :dev},
    49→      {:phoenix_live_view, "~> 1.1.0"},
    50→      {:lazy_html, ">= 0.1.0", only: :test},
    51→      {:phoenix_live_dashboard, "~> 0.8.3"},
    52→      {:esbuild, "~> 0.10", runtime: Mix.env() == :dev},
    53→      {:tailwind, "~> 0.3", runtime: Mix.env() == :dev},
    54→      {:heroicons,
    55→       github: "tailwindlabs/heroicons",
    56→       tag: "v2.2.0",
    57→       sparse: "optimized",
    58→       app: false,
    59→       compile: false,
    60→       depth: 1},
    61→      {:swoosh, "~> 1.16"},
    62→      {:req, "~> 0.5"},
    63→      {:telemetry_metrics, "~> 1.0"},
    64→      {:telemetry_poller, "~> 1.0"},
    65→      {:gettext, "~> 1.0"},
    66→      {:jason, "~> 1.2"},
    67→      {:dns_cluster, "~> 0.2.0"},
    68→      {:bandit, "~> 1.5"}
    69→    ]
    70→  end
    71→
    72→  # Aliases are shortcuts or tasks specific to the current project.
    73→  # For example, to install project dependencies and perform other setup tasks, run:
    74→  #
    75→  #     $ mix setup
    76→  #
    77→  # See the documentation for `Mix` for more info on aliases.
    78→  defp aliases do
    79→    [
    80→      setup: ["deps.get", "ecto.setup", "assets.setup", "assets.build"],
    81→      "ecto.setup": ["ecto.create", "ecto.migrate", "run priv/repo/seeds.exs"],
    82→      "ecto.reset": ["ecto.drop", "ecto.setup"],
    83→      test: ["ecto.create --quiet", "ecto.migrate --quiet", "test"],
    84→      "assets.setup": ["tailwind.install --if-missing", "esbuild.install --if-missing"],
    85→      "assets.build": ["compile", "tailwind todo_list", "esbuild todo_list"],
    86→      "assets.deploy": [
    87→        "tailwind todo_list --minify",
    88→        "esbuild todo_list --minify",
    89→        "phx.digest"
    90→      ],
    91→      precommit: ["compile --warnings-as-errors", "deps.unlock --unused", "format", "test"]
    92→    ]
    93→  end
    94→end
    95→

- Refer to AGENTS.md at the project root for Phoenix-specific coding guidelines

Req is included by default and is the preferred HTTP client for Phoenix apps 195→ 196→### Phoenix v1.8 guidelines 197→ 198→- **Always** begin your LiveView templates with `` which wraps all inner content 199→- The `MyAppWeb.Layouts` module is aliased in the `my_app_web.ex` file, so you can use it without needing to alias it again 200→- Anytime you run into errors with no `current_scope` assign: 201→ - You failed to follow the Authenticated Routes guidelines, or you failed to pass `current_scope` to `` 202→ - **Always** fix the `current_scope` error by moving your routes to the proper `live_session` and ensure you pass `current_scope` as needed 203→- Phoenix v1.8 moved the `<.flash_group>` component to the `Layouts` module. You are **forbidden** from calling `<.flash_group>` outside of the `layouts.ex` module 204→- Out of the box, `core_components.ex` imports an `<.icon name="hero-x-mark" class="w-5 h-5"/>` component for for hero icons. **Always** use the `<.icon>` component for icons, **never** use `Heroicons` modules or similar 205→- **Always** use the imported `<.input>` component for form inputs from `core_components.ex` when available. `<.input>` is imported and using it will save steps and prevent errors 206→- If you override the default input classes (`<.input class="myclass px-2 py-1 rounded-lg">)`) class with your own values, no default classes are inherited, so your 207→custom classes must fully style the input 208→ 209→### JS and CSS guidelines 210→ 211→- **Use Tailwind CSS classes and custom CSS rules** to create polished, responsive, and visually stunning interfaces. 212→- Tailwindcss v4 **no longer needs a tailwind.config.js** and uses a new import syntax in `app.css`: 213→ 214→ @import "tailwindcss" source(none); 215→ @source "../css"; 216→ @source "../js"; 217→ @source "../../lib/my_app_web"; 218→ 219→- **Always use and maintain this import syntax** in the app.css file for projects generated with `phx.new` 220→- **Never** use `@apply` when writing raw css 221→- **Always** manually write your own tailwind-based components instead of using daisyUI for a unique, world-class design 222→- Out of the box **only the app.js and app.css bundles are supported** 223→ - You cannot reference an external vendor'd script `src` or link `href` in the layouts 224→ - You must import the vendor deps into app.js and app.css to use them 225→ - **Never write inline tags within templates** 226→ 227→### UI/UX & design guidelines 228→ 229→- **Produce world-class UI designs** with a focus on usability, aesthetics, and modern design principles 230→- Implement **subtle micro-interactions** (e.g., button hover effects, and smooth transitions) 231→- Ensure **clean typography, spacing, and layout balance** for a refined, premium look 232→- Focus on **delightful details** like hover effects, loading states, and smooth page transitions 233→ 234→ 235→ 236→ 237→ 238→## Elixir guidelines 239→ 240→- Elixir lists **do not support index based access via the access syntax** 241→ 242→ **Never do this (invalid)**: 243→ 244→ i = 0 245→ mylist = ["blue", "green"] 246→ mylist[i] 247→ 248→ Instead, **always** use `Enum.at`, pattern matching, or `List` for index based list access, ie: 249→ 250→ i = 0 251→ mylist = ["blue", "green"] 252→ Enum.at(mylist, i) 253→ 254→- Elixir variables are immutable, but can be rebound, so for block expressions like `if`, `case`, `cond`, etc 255→ you *must* bind the result of the expression to a variable if you want to use it and you CANNOT rebind the result inside the expression, ie: 256→ 257→ # INVALID: we are rebinding inside the `if` and the result never gets assigned 258→ if connected?(socket) do 259→ socket = assign(socket, :val, val) 260→ end 261→ 262→ # VALID: we rebind the result of the `if` to a new variable 263→ socket = 264→ if connected?(socket) do 265→ assign(socket, :val, val) 266→ end 267→ 268→- **Never** nest multiple modules in the same file as it can cause cyclic dependencies and compilation errors 269→- **Never** use map access syntax (`changeset[:field]`) on structs as they do not implement the Access behaviour by default. For regular structs, you **must** access the fields directly, such as `my_struct.field` or use higher level APIs that are available on the struct if they exist, `Ecto.Changeset.get_field/2` for changesets 270→- Elixir's standard library has everything necessary for date and time manipulation. Familiarize yourself with the common `Time`, `Date`, `DateTime`, and `Calendar` interfaces by accessing their documentation as necessary. **Never** install additional dependencies unless asked or for date/time parsing (which you can use the `date_time_parser` package) 271→- Don't use `String.to_atom/1` on user input (memory leak risk) 272→- Predicate function names should not start with `is_` and should end in a question mark. Names like `is_thing` should be reserved for guards 273→- Elixir's builtin OTP primitives like `DynamicSupervisor` and `Registry`, require names in the child spec, such as `{DynamicSupervisor, name: MyApp.MyDynamicSup}`, then you can use `DynamicSupervisor.start_child(MyApp.MyDynamicSup, child_spec)` 274→- Use `Task.async_stream(collection, callback, options)` for concurrent enumeration with back-pressure. The majority of times you will want to pass `timeout: :infinity` as option 275→ 276→## Mix guidelines 277→ 278→- Read the docs and options before using tasks (by using `mix help task_name`) 279→- To debug test failures, run tests in a specific file with `mix test test/my_test.exs` or run all previously failed tests with `mix test --failed` 280→- `mix deps.clean --all` is **almost never needed**. **Avoid** using it unless you have good reason 281→ 282→## Test guidelines 283→ 284→- **Always use `start_supervised!/1`** to start processes in tests as it guarantees cleanup between tests 285→- **Avoid** `Process.sleep/1` and `Process.alive?/1` in tests 286→ - Instead of sleeping to wait for a process to finish, **always** use `Process.monitor/1` and assert on the DOWN message: 287→ 288→ ref = Process.monitor(pid) 289→ assert_receive {:DOWN, ^ref, :process, ^pid, :normal} 290→ 291→ - Instead of sleeping to synchronize before the next call, **always** use `_ = :sys.get_state/1` to ensure the process has handled prior messages 292→ 293→ 294→ 295→## Phoenix guidelines 296→ 297→- Remember Phoenix router `scope` blocks include an optional alias which is prefixed for all routes within the scope. **Always** be mindful of this when creating routes within a scope to avoid duplicate module prefixes. 298→ 299→- You **never** need to create your own `alias` for route definitions! The `scope` provides the alias, ie: 300→ 301→ scope "/admin", AppWeb.Admin do 302→ pipe_through :browser 303→ 304→ live "/users", UserLive, :index 305→ end 306→ 307→ the UserLive route would point to the `AppWeb.Admin.UserLive` module 308→ 309→- `Phoenix.View` no longer is needed or included with Phoenix, don't use it 310→ 311→ 312→ 313→## Ecto Guidelines 314→ 315→- **Always** preload Ecto associations in queries when they'll be accessed in templates, ie a message that needs to reference the `message.user.email` 316→- Remember `import Ecto.Query` and other supporting modules when you write `seeds.exs` 317→- `Ecto.Schema` fields always use the `:string` type, even for `:text`, columns, ie: `field :name, :string` 318→- `Ecto.Changeset.validate_number/2` **DOES NOT SUPPORT the `:allow_nil` option**. By default, Ecto validations only run if a change for the given field exists and the change value is not nil, so such as option is never needed 319→- You **must** use `Ecto.Changeset.get_field(changeset, :field)` to access changeset fields 320→- Fields which are set programatically, such as `user_id`, must not be listed in `cast` calls or similar for security purposes. Instead they must be explicitly set when creating the struct 321→- **Always** invoke `mix ecto.gen.migration migration_name_using_underscores` when generating migration files, so the correct timestamp and conventions are applied 322→ 323→ 324→ 325→## Phoenix HTML guidelines 326→ 327→- Phoenix templates **always** use `~H` or .html.heex files (known as HEEx), **never** use `~E` 328→- **Always** use the imported `Phoenix.Component.form/1` and `Phoenix.Component.inputs_for/1` function to build forms. **Never** use `Phoenix.HTML.form_for` or `Phoenix.HTML.inputs_for` as they are outdated 329→- When building forms **always** use the already imported `Phoenix.Component.to_form/2` (`assign(socket, form: to_form(...))` and `<.form for={@form} id="msg-form">`), then access those forms in the template via `@form[:field]` 330→- **Always** add unique DOM IDs to key elements (like forms, buttons, etc) when writing templates, these IDs can later be used in tests (`<.form for={@form} id="product-form">`) 331→- For "app wide" template imports, you can import/alias into the `my_app_web.ex`'s `html_helpers` block, so they will be available to all LiveViews, LiveComponent's, and all modules that do `use MyAppWeb, :html` (replace "my_app" by the actual app name) 332→ 333→- Elixir supports `if/else` but **does NOT support `if/else if` or `if/elsif`**. **Never use `else if` or `elseif` in Elixir**, **always** use `cond` or `case` for multiple conditionals. 334→ 335→ **Never do this (invalid)**: 336→ 337→ <%= if condition do %> 338→ ... 339→ <% else if other_condition %> 340→ ... 341→ <% end %> 342→ 343→ Instead **always** do this: 344→ 345→ <%= cond do %> 346→ <% condition -> %> 347→ ... 348→ <% condition2 -> %> 349→ ... 350→ <% true -> %> 351→ ... 352→ <% end %> 353→ 354→- HEEx require special tag annotation if you want to insert literal curly's like `{` or `}`. If you want to show a textual code snippet on the page in a `

   404→## Phoenix LiveView guidelines
   405→
   406→- **Never** use the deprecated `live_redirect` and `live_patch` functions, instead **always** use the `<.link navigate={href}>` and  `<.link patch={href}>` in templates, and `push_navigate` and `push_patch` functions LiveViews
   407→- **Avoid LiveComponent's** unless you have a strong, specific need for them
   408→- LiveViews should be named like `AppWeb.WeatherLive`, with a `Live` suffix. When you go to add LiveView routes to the router, the default `:browser` scope is **already aliased** with the `AppWeb` module, so you can just do `live "/weather", WeatherLive`
   409→
   410→### LiveView streams
   411→
   412→- **Always** use LiveView streams for collections for assigning regular lists to avoid memory ballooning and runtime termination with the following operations:
   413→  - basic append of N items - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg])`
   414→  - resetting stream with new items - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg], reset: true)` (e.g. for filtering items)
   415→  - prepend to stream - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg], at: -1)`
   416→  - deleting items - `stream_delete(socket, :messages, msg)`
   417→
   418→- When using the `stream/3` interfaces in the LiveView, the LiveView template must 1) always set `phx-update="stream"` on the parent element, with a DOM id on the parent element like `id="messages"` and 2) consume the `@streams.stream_name` collection and use the id as the DOM id for each child. For a call like `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg])` in the LiveView, the template would be:
   419→
   420→      

   421→        

   422→          {msg.text}
   423→        

   424→      

   425→
   426→- LiveView streams are *not* enumerable, so you cannot use `Enum.filter/2` or `Enum.reject/2` on them. Instead, if you want to filter, prune, or refresh a list of items on the UI, you **must refetch the data and re-stream the entire stream collection, passing reset: true**:
   427→
   428→      def handle_event("filter", %{"filter" => filter}, socket) do
   429→        # re-fetch the messages based on the filter
   430→        messages = list_messages(filter)
   431→
   432→        {:noreply,
   433→         socket
   434→         |> assign(:messages_empty?, messages == [])
   435→         # reset the stream with the new messages
   436→         |> stream(:messages, messages, reset: true)}
   437→      end
   438→
   439→- LiveView streams *do not support counting or empty states*. If you need to display a count, you must track it using a separate assign. For empty states, you can use Tailwind classes:
   440→
   441→      

   442→        
No tasks yet

   443→        

   444→          {task.name}
   445→        

   446→      

   447→
   448→  The above only works if the empty state is the only HTML block alongside the stream for-comprehension.
   449→
   450→- When updating an assign that should change content inside any streamed item(s), you MUST re-stream the items
   451→  along with the updated assign:
   452→
   453→      def handle_event("edit_message", %{"message_id" => message_id}, socket) do
   454→        message = Chat.get_message!(message_id)
   455→        edit_form = to_form(Chat.change_message(message, %{content: message.content}))
   456→
   457→        # re-insert message so @editing_message_id toggle logic takes effect for that stream item
   458→        {:noreply,
   459→         socket
   460→         |> stream_insert(:messages, message)
   461→         |> assign(:editing_message_id, String.to_integer(message_id))
   462→         |> assign(:edit_form, edit_form)}
   463→      end
   464→
   465→  And in the template:
   466→
   467→      

   468→        

   469→          {message.username}
   470→          <%= if @editing_message_id == message.id do %>
   471→            <%!-- Edit mode --%>
   472→            <.form for={@edit_form} id="edit-form-#{message.id}" phx-submit="save_edit">
   473→              ...
   474→            
   475→          <% end %>
   476→        

   477→      

   478→
   479→- **Never** use the deprecated `phx-update="append"` or `phx-update="prepend"` for collections
   480→
   481→### LiveView JavaScript interop
   482→
   483→- Remember anytime you use `phx-hook="MyHook"` and that JS hook manages its own DOM, you **must** also set the `phx-update="ignore"` attribute
   484→- **Always** provide an unique DOM id alongside `phx-hook` otherwise a compiler error will be raised
   485→
   486→LiveView hooks come in two flavors, 1) colocated js hooks for "inline" scripts defined inside HEEx,
   487→and 2) external `phx-hook` annotations where JavaScript object literals are defined and passed to the `LiveSocket` constructor.
   488→
   489→#### Inline colocated js hooks
   490→
   491→**Never** write raw embedded `
   508→
   509→- colocated hooks are automatically integrated into the app.js bundle
   510→- colocated hooks names **MUST ALWAYS** start with a `.` prefix, i.e. `.PhoneNumber`
   511→
   512→#### External phx-hook
   513→
   514→External JS hooks (`
`) must be placed in `assets/js/` and passed to the
   515→LiveSocket constructor:
   516→
   517→    const MyHook = {
   518→      mounted() { ... }
   519→    }
   520→    let liveSocket = new LiveSocket("/live", Socket, {
   521→      hooks: { MyHook }
   522→    });
   523→
   524→#### Pushing events between client and server
   525→
   526→Use LiveView's `push_event/3` when you need to push events/data to the client for a phx-hook to handle.
   527→**Always** return or rebind the socket on `push_event/3` when pushing events:
   528→
   529→    # re-bind socket so we maintain event state to be pushed
   530→    socket = push_event(socket, "my_event", %{...})
   531→
   532→    # or return the modified socket directly:
   533→    def handle_event("some_event", _, socket) do
   534→      {:noreply, push_event(socket, "my_event", %{...})}
   535→    end
   536→
   537→Pushed events can then be picked up in a JS hook with `this.handleEvent`:
   538→
   539→    mounted() {
   540→      this.handleEvent("my_event", data => console.log("from server:", data));
   541→    }
   542→
   543→Clients can also push an event to the server and receive a reply with `this.pushEvent`:
   544→
   545→    mounted() {
   546→      this.el.addEventListener("click", e => {
   547→        this.pushEvent("my_event", { one: 1 }, reply => console.log("got reply from server:", reply));
   548→      })
   549→    }
   550→
   551→Where the server handled it via:
   552→
   553→    def handle_event("my_event", %{"one" => 1}, socket) do
   554→      {:reply, %{two: 2}, socket}
   555→    end
   556→
   557→### LiveView tests
   558→
   559→- `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` module and `LazyHTML` (included) for making your assertions
   560→- Form tests are driven by `Phoenix.LiveViewTest`'s `render_submit/2` and `render_change/2` functions
   561→- Come up with a step-by-step test plan that splits major test cases into small, isolated files. You may start with simpler tests that verify content exists, gradually add interaction tests
   562→- **Always reference the key element IDs you added in the LiveView templates in your tests** for `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` functions like `element/2`, `has_element/2`, selectors, etc
   563→- **Never** tests again raw HTML, **always** use `element/2`, `has_element/2`, and similar: `assert has_element?(view, "#my-form")`
   564→- Instead of relying on testing text content, which can change, favor testing for the presence of key elements
   565→- Focus on testing outcomes rather than implementation details
   566→- Be aware that `Phoenix.Component` functions like `<.form>` might produce different HTML than expected. Test against the output HTML structure, not your mental model of what you expect it to be
   567→- When facing test failures with element selectors, add debug statements to print the actual HTML, but use `LazyHTML` selectors to limit the output, ie:
   568→
   569→      html = render(view)
   570→      document = LazyHTML.from_fragment(html)
   571→      matches = LazyHTML.filter(document, "your-complex-selector")
   572→      IO.inspect(matches, label: "Matches")
   573→
   574→### Form handling
   575→
   576→#### Creating a form from params
   577→
   578→If you want to create a form based on `handle_event` params:
   579→
   580→    def handle_event("submitted", params, socket) do
   581→      {:noreply, assign(socket, form: to_form(params))}
   582→    end
   583→
   584→When you pass a map to `to_form/1`, it assumes said map contains the form params, which are expected to have string keys.
   585→
   586→You can also specify a name to nest the params:
   587→
   588→    def handle_event("submitted", %{"user" => user_params}, socket) do
   589→      {:noreply, assign(socket, form: to_form(user_params, as: :user))}
   590→    end
   591→
   592→#### Creating a form from changesets
   593→
   594→When using changesets, the underlying data, form params, and errors are retrieved from it. The `:as` option is automatically computed too. E.g. if you have a user schema:
   595→
   596→    defmodule MyApp.Users.User do
   597→      use Ecto.Schema
   598→      ...
   599→    end
   600→
   601→And then you create a changeset that you pass to `to_form`:
   602→
   603→    %MyApp.Users.User{}
   604→    |> Ecto.Changeset.change()
   605→    |> to_form()
   606→
   607→Once the form is submitted, the params will be available under `%{"user" => user_params}`.
   608→
   609→In the template, the form form assign can be passed to the `<.form>` function component:
   610→
   611→    <.form for={@form} id="todo-form" phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save">
   612→      <.input field={@form[:field]} type="text" />
   613→    
   614→
   615→Always give the form an explicit, unique DOM ID, like `id="todo-form"`.
   616→
   617→#### Avoiding form errors
   618→

   620→

   622→    <.form for={@form} id="my-form">

   624→    
   625→
   626→And **never** do this:
   627→
   628→    <%!-- NEVER do this (invalid) --%>
   629→    <.form for={@changeset} id="my-form">
   630→      <.input field={@changeset[:field]} type="text" />
   631→    
   632→
   633→- You are FORBIDDEN from accessing the changeset in the template as it will cause errors
   634→- **Never** use `<.form let={f} ...>` in the template, instead **always use `<.form for={@form} ...>`**, then drive all form references from the form assign as in `@form[:field]`. The UI should **always** be driven by a `to_form/2` assigned in the LiveView module that is derived from a changeset

You are facilitating architecture decisions for an event-sourced Phoenix/LiveView todo list application.

## Project Context

**Project path:** /home/jwilger/projects/todo_list
**Project type:** Elixir/Phoenix 1.8 web application (currently a fresh Phoenix app with no event sourcing dependencies)
**Purpose:** Demonstration/learning project — intentionally over-engineered to showcase SDLC patterns

## Current State

The project has:
- A complete event model with 1 workflow (Task Management) containing 8 vertical slices
- Domain discovery complete
- GWT scenarios for all slices
- A fresh Phoenix 1.8 app with Ecto, PostgreSQL, LiveView (see mix.exs)
- NO event sourcing libraries yet
- NO `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` yet

## Domain Overview

Single-user prioritized todo list. One actor (User), no authentication. Events:
- TaskAdded, TaskCompleted, TaskReopened, TaskDescriptionChanged, TaskReprioritized, TaskDeleted

Commands: AddTask, CompleteTask, ReopenTask, ChangeTaskDescription, ReprioritizeTask, DeleteTask

Read Models: ActiveTaskList (main view), CompletedTaskList (completed view)

No automations, no external integrations.

## Event Model Files

All event model documentation is at:
- `docs/event_model/domain/overview.md`
- `docs/event_model/workflows/task-management/overview.md`
- `docs/event_model/workflows/task-management/slices/*.md` (8 slices)

## What You Need To Do

Work through architecture decisions for this project across these categories:
1. **Technology Stack** — ES framework, event store, projections, UUID generation
2. **Domain Boundaries** — Aggregate design, context module API, domain types
3. **Integration Approaches** — LiveView integration pattern
4. **Cross-Cutting Concerns** — Testing strategy, error handling

First, read the event model files to understand the domain fully. Then present your identified decision points to me for review. I may have pre-made decisions or want to add/remove items.

**CRITICAL**: After I approve the decision points, work through them ONE AT A TIME. For each decision:
1. Present options with tradeoffs
2. Get my choice
3. Invoke `/sdlc:adr decide <topic>` (via the Skill tool) to update ARCHITECTURE.md and create an ADR PR

Each decision = one ADR PR. Do NOT batch multiple decisions.

## Important Notes

- This is an Elixir/Phoenix project — all technology choices should be from the Elixir ecosystem
- The project uses Nix for development environment, docker-compose for PostgreSQL
- Tests should use in-memory adapters where possible (fast tests)
- The project already uses git-spice for stacked PRs (see `.claude/sdlc.yaml`)
- Refer to AGENTS.md at the project root for Phoenix-specific coding guidelines

content.text:
I now have a thorough understanding of the event model and project setup. Let me present the decision agenda.

---

## Architecture Decision Agenda

I have reviewed the complete event model (1 workflow, 8 slices, 6 events, 6 commands, 2 read models, no automations, no external integrations). Here are the decision points I have identified, organized by category.

### Category 1: Technology Stack

**1.1 Event Sourcing Framework**
Why it matters: This is the foundation for command dispatch, event persistence, and aggregate lifecycle. The Elixir ecosystem has a few options with very different approaches.

**1.2 Event Store Backend**
Why it matters: Where events are durably stored. Affects operational complexity, test setup, and projection mechanisms.

**1.3 Projection Mechanism**
Why it matters: How events become queryable read models (ActiveTaskList, CompletedTaskList). Determines consistency guarantees and code structure.

**1.4 UUID Generation**
Why it matters: Task IDs need to be generated somewhere (aggregate? command handler?). Choosing a UUID library and strategy affects testability.

### Category 2: Domain Architecture

**2.1 Aggregate Design**
Why it matters: The event model has a single "Task" concept. We need to decide if there is one aggregate per task or a single "TaskList" aggregate, and where domain logic lives (execute/apply callbacks vs. separate domain layer).

**2.2 Domain Types**
Why it matters: The event model references types like "NonEmptyString" for descriptions. We need to decide whether to use dedicated domain value types or raw Elixir primitives with validation in the aggregate.

**2.3 Context Module API**
Why it matters: LiveView needs a clean boundary to dispatch commands and query read models. The context module shape determines how the UI layer interacts with the domain.

### Category 3: Integration Patterns

**3.1 LiveView Integration**
Why it matters: How LiveView dispatches commands and receives read model updates. Affects whether the UI gets real-time updates (PubSub) or polls/re-queries after commands.

### Category 4: Cross-Cutting Concerns

**4.1 Testing Strategy**
Why it matters: Determines test layers (aggregate unit, projection unit, LiveView integration), test adapter choice (in-memory vs. real event store), and how GWT scenarios map to tests.

**4.2 Error Handling**
Why it matters: Command rejections (e.g., "Task is already completed") need to surface in the UI. The error representation pattern affects both domain and LiveView code.

---

### Items I am NOT including (and why):

- **Authentication/Authorization** -- The domain explicitly has no auth (single user, no login).
- **Service decomposition / bounded contexts** -- Single bounded context, single workflow, monolith is the obvious and only choice.
- **Messaging / async communication** -- No automations, no integrations, no need for a message bus.
- **Observability** -- Demo/learning project; standard Phoenix telemetry is sufficient without a decision.
- **Anti-corruption layers** -- No external integrations.

---

Please review this agenda:
- **Add** any decisions I missed
- **Remove** any you consider out of scope or already settled
- **Modify** the framing of any decision
- **Provide pre-made decisions** for any items you have already decided -- I will record those immediately via ADR without going through full facilitation

Good, the agenda is approved as-is. Let me proceed with the first decision point.

---

## Decision 1.1: Event Sourcing Framework

**Context:** This project needs a CQRS/ES framework to handle command dispatch, aggregate lifecycle management, event persistence, and projection building. The Elixir ecosystem has a few mature options, each with a different philosophy.

Here are the realistic options:

**Option A: Commanded**
The most mature and widely-used CQRS/ES framework in Elixir. Provides a full opinionated stack: command dispatch via a router, aggregate modules with `execute/2` and `apply/2` callbacks, middleware pipeline, process managers, and an event store adapter system. Has companion libraries for Ecto projections (`commanded_ecto_projections`) and EventStore (`commanded_eventstore_adapter`). Well-documented with an active community. The tradeoff is that it is a large framework with conventions you must follow -- it is opinionated about how aggregates, commands, and events are structured.

**Option B: Incident**
A lighter-weight event sourcing library that focuses on the core event sourcing pattern without the full CQRS router/middleware stack. Less opinionated, giving you more control over how commands are dispatched and how the pipeline is structured. Smaller community and less ecosystem support (no built-in projection library). You would need to build more plumbing yourself.

**Option C: Custom (hand-rolled)**
Build event sourcing from scratch using GenServer/Agent for aggregates, a custom events table in PostgreSQL, and manual projection logic. Maximum flexibility, zero framework lock-in, full understanding of every line. The tradeoff is significant implementation effort for infrastructure that is not the point of this project, and you lose battle-tested concurrency/snapshotting/subscription logic.

Which approach fits your needs for this project?

input.command:
gh pr create --title "ADR: Use Commanded as CQRS/ES Framework" --label "adr" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Context

The Todo List application is an event-sourced Phoenix/LiveView application that needs a CQRS/ES framework to handle command dispatch, aggregate lifecycle management, event persistence, and projection building. The Elixir ecosystem has several options with different tradeoffs.

## Decision

Use **Commanded** as the CQRS/ES framework for the Todo List application.

Commanded provides:
- Command dispatch via a router with middleware pipeline
- Aggregate modules with `execute/2` and `apply/2` callbacks
- Process managers for cross-aggregate coordination (not needed now, available later)
- An adapter system for event store backends
- Companion libraries for Ecto projections and EventStore integration

## Alternatives Considered

### Incident (lightweight ES library)
- **Pro:** Less opinionated, more control over pipeline structure
- **Con:** Smaller community, less ecosystem support, no built-in projection library
- **Con:** Would require building more plumbing (command routing, middleware, etc.)
- **Rejected because:** The additional flexibility does not justify the missing ecosystem for this project

### Custom / hand-rolled
- **Pro:** Maximum flexibility, zero framework lock-in, full understanding of every line
- **Con:** Significant implementation effort for infrastructure that is not the point of this project
- **Con:** Loses battle-tested concurrency, snapshotting, and subscription logic
- **Rejected because:** Building ES infrastructure from scratch is not the learning goal; using it effectively is

## Consequences

### Positive
- Rich ecosystem: `commanded_ecto_projections` and `commanded_eventstore_adapter` provide ready-made solutions for projections and event storage
- Well-documented with active community and established patterns
- Aggregate lifecycle (snapshotting, concurrency) handled by the framework
- Clear, opinionated structure maps well to event model slices (one aggregate, commands, events)

### Negative
- Framework conventions must be followed (opinionated structure for aggregates, commands, events)
- Additional dependency weight compared to hand-rolled approach
- Learning curve for Commanded-specific patterns (router, middleware, application module)

## References

- [Commanded GitHub](https://github.com/commanded/commanded)
- [Commanded guides](https://hexdocs.pm/commanded/)
- Event model: `docs/event_model/`
EOF
)"

content.text:
## Decision 1.2: Event Store Backend

**Context:** With Commanded chosen as the CQRS/ES framework (ADR PR #3), we need to decide where events are durably stored. Commanded uses an adapter system, so the event store backend is a separate choice. This decision also covers what adapter is used in tests vs. dev/prod.

Here are the options:

**Option A: PostgreSQL via EventStore + commanded_eventstore_adapter (with in-memory for tests)**
Uses the `eventstore` Elixir library backed by PostgreSQL for dev and production. This means a second PostgreSQL database alongside the existing Ecto application database. The `commanded_eventstore_adapter` bridges Commanded to this store. Tests use Commanded's built-in in-memory adapter for speed (no database needed, fast isolation). This is the most common Commanded deployment pattern. Tradeoff: two PostgreSQL databases to manage in dev (docker-compose already runs Postgres), but operational simplicity since both use the same database engine.

**Option B: PostgreSQL via EventStore for everything (including tests)**
Same as Option A for dev/prod, but tests also use the real PostgreSQL event store. Provides higher fidelity testing (tests hit the same storage as production). Tradeoff: tests are slower, require database setup/teardown, and are harder to run in isolation. Given the project is a learning/demo app, the speed penalty may not be worth the fidelity gain.

**Option C: In-memory adapter only (no persistent event store)**
Use Commanded's in-memory adapter for everything, including dev. Events exist only in process memory and are lost on restart. Simplest setup -- no extra database, no EventStore dependency. Tradeoff: events vanish on server restart, which undermines the entire point of event sourcing for a demo project. Useful only if you never need to restart the dev server with persisted state.

Which event store backend approach fits your needs?

content.text:
## Decision 1.2: Event Store Backend

**Context:** With Commanded chosen as the CQRS/ES framework (ADR PR #3), we need to decide where events are durably stored. Commanded uses an adapter system, so the event store backend is a separate choice. This decision also covers what adapter is used in tests vs. dev/prod.

Here are the options:

**Option A: PostgreSQL via EventStore + commanded_eventstore_adapter (with in-memory for tests)**
Uses the `eventstore` Elixir library backed by PostgreSQL for dev and production. This means a second PostgreSQL database alongside the existing Ecto application database. The `commanded_eventstore_adapter` bridges Commanded to this store. Tests use Commanded's built-in in-memory adapter for speed (no database needed, fast isolation). This is the most common Commanded deployment pattern. Tradeoff: two PostgreSQL databases to manage in dev (docker-compose already runs Postgres), but operational simplicity since both use the same database engine.

**Option B: PostgreSQL via EventStore for everything (including tests)**
Same as Option A for dev/prod, but tests also use the real PostgreSQL event store. Provides higher fidelity testing (tests hit the same storage as production). Tradeoff: tests are slower, require database setup/teardown, and are harder to run in isolation. Given the project is a learning/demo app, the speed penalty may not be worth the fidelity gain.

**Option C: In-memory adapter only (no persistent event store)**
Use Commanded's in-memory adapter for everything, including dev. Events exist only in process memory and are lost on restart. Simplest setup -- no extra database, no EventStore dependency. Tradeoff: events vanish on server restart, which undermines the entire point of event sourcing for a demo project. Useful only if you never need to restart the dev server with persisted state.

Which event store backend approach fits your needs?

## Decision 1.2: Event Store Backend

**Option A: PostgreSQL via EventStore + in-memory for tests**
The `eventstore` library backed by PostgreSQL for dev/prod, with Commanded's built-in in-memory adapter for tests. Most common Commanded pattern. Means a second PostgreSQL database alongside your Ecto app DB (both served by the same docker-compose Postgres). Tests stay fast with no DB dependency.

**Option B: PostgreSQL via EventStore for everything (including tests)**
Same as A for dev/prod, but tests also hit the real PostgreSQL event store. Higher fidelity, but slower tests and more setup overhead. Questionable value for a demo project.

**Option C: In-memory adapter only (no persistence)**
In-memory everywhere, including dev. Simplest possible setup. Events vanish on restart, which rather defeats the purpose of event sourcing. A philosophical contradiction, if you will.

---

Which approach?

content.thinking:
The user wants to use stacked PRs (via git-spice) for the ADRs, so each subsequent ADR PR builds on top of the previous one rather than being independent branches off main. This is a good point - the ARCHITECTURE.md file will be built up incrementally, and each decision adds to it. With stacked PRs, each new branch can include the content from previous decisions.

Let me pass this instruction to the design-facilitator agent along with the user's choice for 1.2 (they haven't actually chosen yet - they gave feedback about the process, not the decision). Let me re-read... they said "When creating multiple ADRs, we should use stacked PRs so that each one can build on the context of the previous one." This is process feedback, not a decision on 1.2.


I see the project already has git-spice configured in sdlc.yaml, so I should make sure the facilitator stacks subsequent ADR branches on top of previous ones going forward. I'll acknowledge their feedback about the stacked PR approach and still ask for their actual decision on 1.2.

content.text:
A sensible observation. Each decision builds on the previous ones, so `ARCHITECTURE.md` should accumulate rather than conflict. The project is already configured for git-spice — I'll make sure the facilitator stacks each subsequent ADR branch on top of the last. No more parallel branches diverging into the void like... well, like everything else in this universe.

Now — you still haven't told me which event store backend you'd like. Option A, B, or C?

message.content: Option A, and make sure the facilitator also stacks *this* PR on the previous one (#3)

input.prompt:
The user chose **Option A: PostgreSQL via EventStore + commanded_eventstore_adapter (with in-memory for tests)** for the Event Store Backend.

**CRITICAL PROCESS CHANGE**: The user wants all ADR PRs to use **stacked PRs via git-spice** so each decision builds on the previous one. This project uses git-spice (`gs` commands). 

For THIS decision and ALL future decisions:
1. Stack the new branch ON TOP of the previous ADR branch (PR #3's branch)
2. Use `gs branch create <name>` to create a stacked branch (it automatically stacks on the current branch)
3. First, check out the branch from PR #3, then create the new branch on top of it

To find PR #3's branch name:
```bash
gh pr view 3 --json headRefName -q .headRefName
```

Then:
```bash
git checkout <that-branch>
gs branch create adr/event-store-backend
```

Now invoke `/sdlc:adr decide` via the Skill tool to record this decision. BUT make sure the adr skill uses the stacked branch approach. The topic is "event-store-backend" and the decision is:

- Use `eventstore` library + `commanded_eventstore_adapter` for PostgreSQL-based event storage in dev/prod
- Use Commanded's built-in in-memory adapter for tests (fast, no DB dependency)
- This means two PostgreSQL databases: one for Ecto (application data/projections) and one for the event store
- Both served by the same docker-compose PostgreSQL instance
- Alternatives considered: PostgreSQL for tests too (slower, unnecessary for demo), in-memory only (defeats purpose of ES)

After the ADR is created, stop and report back so we can proceed to decision 1.3.

1→# Architecture
     2→
     3→> This is the authoritative source for the current architecture of the Todo List application.
     4→> For the rationale behind each decision, see the ADR PRs labeled `adr` on GitHub.
     5→
     6→## Technology Stack
     7→
     8→### Event Sourcing Framework: Commanded
     9→
    10→The application uses [Commanded](https://github.com/commanded/commanded) as its
    11→CQRS/ES framework. Commanded provides the full command dispatch pipeline,
    12→aggregate lifecycle management, event persistence, and projection infrastructure.
    13→
    14→Key components from the Commanded ecosystem:
    15→
    16→- **commanded** -- Core framework (command router, aggregate callbacks, middleware)
    17→- **commanded_eventstore_adapter** -- Event store adapter (decision pending on backend)
    18→- **commanded_ecto_projections** -- Ecto-based read model projections (decision pending)
    19→
    20→Aggregates implement domain logic via `execute/2` (command handling) and `apply/2`
    21→(state mutation) callbacks. Commands are dispatched through a Commanded router.
    22→

- **commanded_eventstore_adapter** -- Bridges Commanded to the EventStore backend
- **commanded_ecto_projections** -- Ecto-based read model projections (decision pending)

Aggregates implement domain logic via `execute/2` (command handling) and `apply/2`
(state mutation) callbacks. Commands are dispatched through a Commanded router.

### Event Store Backend: PostgreSQL (EventStore) + In-Memory for Tests

Events are durably stored in PostgreSQL using the
[eventstore](https://github.com/commanded/eventstore) library with the
`commanded_eventstore_adapter`. This requires a dedicated PostgreSQL database
separate from the main Ecto application database.

**Two PostgreSQL databases** (same docker-compose instance):

- **Application database** (Ecto) -- Projections / read models, standard Phoenix data
- **Event store database** (EventStore) -- Append-only event streams

**Test environment:** Tests use Commanded's built-in in-memory event store adapter.
This keeps tests fast (no database I/O), isolated (no shared state between tests),
and free of infrastructure dependencies.
